sentences.text: My name is Rose Minsky.
I'm 75 years old.
My name is Rose Minsky.
I was born October 28, 1919.
In Warsaw, Poland.
Well, I had a-- a happy childhood.
I had a sister, and I had two brothers.
I went to Polish school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a tailor.
I was the oldest.
I had to help out with the younger children.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I didn't play too much.
I had responsibilities, like I said, as the oldest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I brought up, practically, my youngest brother.
Because by the time he was born, my mother was already a sickly woman.
So I helped a lot with bringing him up.
That's why I still suffer from the loss of him more than from the other siblings.
Because I was very much attached to him.
The old-- the youngest was Motel.
He was born-- um, 1931. 1931.
My older brother was born in 1926.
And my sister was born in 1923.
My other both was Yankel.
And my sister was Chaya.
Very happy.
Having a sister and having two brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very much attached to each other.
We were a very close family.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I went to Polish school.
And I learned very fast, speaking Polish.
But my father wanted us to speak Jewish.
And my mother, they want us to continue with the Jewish language.
That's why the younger children went already to a-- to a Jewish school.
That means that they gave-- it was in Polish too.
But they continued with the Jewish education.
My parents, they were tailors, both.
They worked in their own house.
My father was doing custom made suits and coats for ladies.
My mother was helping.
Even I was helping, because it was very expensive to have hired help.
In the season, he had two helpers, paid.
But we tried to help too, because we want to have a little bit of a better life, like be able to go some in the country.
So we helped a lot, me and my mother, in the business, in the tailoring business.
He was considered one of the most qualified persons to do like a trousseau for a bride.
From a small town, she came to Poland, to Warsaw.
She stayed even a week in a hotel to be able to get the-- the coat done by my father.
That's what a name he had.
Very talented man.
He could copy a fashion from a store and make the same thing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother also.
She-- she used to sew for us clothing.
She never bought us.
When we  were children, our dresses she made.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to live very economically, of course, to be able to-- to-- to have the children to buy a bike for a child, to buy ice skates.
So we had to help out, otherwise we couldn't have those things.
And we did.
My father's name is Simcha.
Matter of fact, my son is named after him.
Simcha.
My mother's name was Tobi.
(LAUGHING) Most devoted parents in the whole world.
My father and my mother worked until 3 o'clock in the morning to be able to get a place in the summer for us to be in the country.
To be able to send me ice skating, to buy me ice skates, to buy-- to buy the bike for the brothers, for us.
They worked very hard.
That's why in 1938, I said to them, I said, you never go on vacation.
We were already big enough to take care of ourselves, me and my sister.
I said, take the two little boys, that they shouldn't bother us.
Take them, and go with them on a vacation for 10 days.
And they did.
This was the first vacation, in 1938, a year before the war, that they took.
They went to a town-- a resort town, Zaborow.
It's-- it's a famous town for people with bad breathing problems, with the airs over there, beautiful.
Even now people go there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I experienced in school a lot of anti-semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my-- my parents managed to take me out from this school.
They put me to a school where we lived nearby, in the Jewish neighborhood.
Was a Jew-- with Jewish teachers, but it was a public school.
It-- I felt better in that school.
And I remember that I was invited to a birthday party, to another-- to a girl from the school, who lived in the-- in the Gentile section.
And we were there-- just from our looks, because we were brunette, dark eyes, dark hair-- the-- the Christian children start calling us that we're Jews and throwing stones after us.
This I never forget.
A matter of fact, that I threw one stone back, and it got a boy in-- in the head.
A boy who threw at us this stone.
And I was afraid they're going to-- because I saw from far away that he was bleeding.
And I started running home.
I run up on the third floor, without breath.
My mother said, what happened?
I said please, lock the door, because they're going to kill me.
I think I-- I wanted to maybe I killed that boy.
[LAUGHING] He throw that-- the stones, and I did it back.
And I was afraid for two weeks to go out in the street.
Did you see that boy again?
No.
There was.
There was.
They didn't let us play games, ballgames.
They didn't let us.
Luckily, I lived in the Jewish neighborhood.
So I had nothing in common to them.
And we avoided to go to the-- to the Gentile section.
We could-- there was a beautiful park, the Saxony Park was the name.
And we couldn't go in in that park.
Because that park was always-- they-- they didn't let us in, because we were Jewish.
Polish students from-- from universities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with sticks, with some kind of things in their hands to hurt us.
Matter of fact, in 1938, there was supposed to be a war with Lithuania at that time.
Lithuania was claiming that part of the border, the city is theirs.
And the Poles says it's theirs.
There was supposed to be a war over-- over that thing.
So of course, my father worked very hard.
He had a few zlotys-- zlotys, that means like dollars, the money-- saved up in the bank.
So everybody run to the bank and want to withdraw his money to prepare for the-- if it's going to be a war, some should be food in the house for the children.
He stood, there was a big-- a big line in the street.
And the Polish students, they went out of the school, of the-- of the university, and they start hollering, 'All the Jews got the money.
It's how-- it's them, it's their money.
The Jews got their money in the bank.' And they start beating up.
And they beat up my father so badly that our neighbor had a corset store on the street.
And she saw my father laying in the gutter, bleeding.
And they meant to-- to-- to call a street-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --car with a-- with a horse, you know, those cars.
And they put him on that car, and they brought him home.
We had to call our doctor.
He was a week in bed, very, very, very beaten up.
Blue, all blue.
This I remember.
Those kind of things I remember.
This was in 1938, a war-- a year before the war broke out, not from the Germans, but from the Poles.
The reaction was that my mother was writing letters to her brother, which was living here in-- in-- in New York, and begging him to do something, to strain himself to do.
Whatever it's going to cost, we'll be able to work and pay back, to bring us over, to-- to do the legalities, do the papers.
And to bring us over to America.
That she got four nice kids, but they have no future.
She got-- there will be no future for them in Poland.
But of course, the brother, especially his wife, they didn't pay so much attention to it.
They told her-- the answer was, their letters was that it's very hard in America to find a job and to find an apartment.
And if your husband makes a living over there, so you better sit over there.
This was the answer.
That they didn't realize that it's that bad.
They thought that maybe my mother exaggerates.
After that, it was not for a long time.
Because soon after that, the war broke out.
And there was no-- no correspondence during the war.
Yeah, I lived in the Jewish area.
The Jewish area was like a Jewish town.
There was a lot of Hasidic Jews.
And there were a lot of free Jews, not Hasidic Jews.
They had their own institutions.
Somebody took sick, we could go to a Jewish clinic that was supported by Jews.
They had-- like a girl, a poor girl was getting married, and she had no money.
My father used to collect money for that purpose, to give her-- to have-- to give her something to buy something.
It was-- we lived very close.
One helped the other.
It wouldn't be-- in the ghetto time, we would be right away wiped out, if not for this.
There were soup kitchens.
There were orphanages.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were Jewish hospitals.
We-- we didn't have too much need to be with them, with the-- with the Polish people, because we had-- had everything.
We had groups, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] self-educating groups, that we educated ourselves.
We had our libraries.
Libraries.
We had theaters, plays.
We had a full life between our own people.
So we didn't miss too much.
The-- the acceptance.
They wouldn't accept us anyway.
So we had to create everything on our own.
Yes, oh yes.
My father was-- loved, he loved very much the Jewish theater.
And he loved- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a music lover.
Then when he was sitting and doing his work, the radio had to play, all the time, classical music.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- as a matter of fact, I have that article that I gave you to read about it, about the radio.
In 1938, shortly before the war broke out, he bought an expensive radio.
At that time, it was, for a tailor, too much money to spend.
My mother was arguing with him.
She wanted to spend the money for clothes and for shoes for the children.
But he set his mind.
It was a German name, Telefunken.
The first invention of talking radios.
Before, we were listening on the ear phones.
This was the first one that could speak aloud.
And this was that time 600 zlotys.
It's like $600.
And my mother said, no.
We cannot afford to do this.
But he bought-- he bought it on installments.
And-- and we had it only for one year.
It was a beautiful, big cabinet.
We could hear auditions from the-- from all Europe, from all the countries.
We had a lot of enjoyment listening to this-- to this radio.
But then Hitler came out in-- came in in September, 1939.
We had to give up everything to the Germans.
Fur coats, radios, jewelry, money.
Everything all of a sudden became their property.
So my father-- we had to pay-- we paid up already the radio.
We had to pay one more installment.
And my father said, I won't give them this radio.
But he couldn't hide it either.
It was a big cabinet.
So my mother came up with an idea to take the radio down to the basement and to chop it up in small pieces and let it go down in the drain in the sewer.
That's what we did.
And we all started crying.
We loved that radio so much.
We had so much enjoyment.
And all of a sudden, my father stays with an ax.
I still can see it, the way he chops the radio.
So my father said, don't worry, children.
Don't cry.
If we're going to survive, if we're going to live, there will be another radio.
That was his last-- his words.
Was your family religious?
Not orthodox religious, but my parents observed the Jewish tradition, holidays, and you know, like Hanukkah, Purim.
Saturdays, my mother made my father to go to shul.
We celebrated the-- Hanukkah.
We invited the rest of the family.
My mother was doing the-- the latkes.
We were singing Hanukkah songs.
My father was lighting candles.
It was very festive.
More-- more in a festive way than here.
I didn't have really-- my father hired a known rabbi to teach us, me and my sister, to teach us Jewish prayers.
We weren't too much interested, because we were-- especially me, I like to learn things that I could understand.
He told us to say the prayers, and I didn't know what-- what the meaning of it, he didn't explain.
An old-- 80-years-old rabbi was teaching us.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- we disliked, he didn't want the rabbi to come no more to teach us.
But in the house, we spoke Jewish.
We couldn't speak.
I learned the [INAUDIBLE].
When I got married, my husband, Nathan, taught me more.
He's well-educated and Jewish.
And by now, I can read.
I can read a Jewish newspaper, a Jewish book.
I only cannot read the Hebrew.
When it comes to the Hebrew, I skip the words, and I read again.
[LAUGHS]
The Sabbath.
My mother lighted candles.
We had the dinner altogether, of course.
We each made chicken soup and-- and fish, all the traditional food for Shabbos.
And we sang.
My father liked to sing.
We sang around the table those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish songs.
It was a-- it was a festival, you know, a feeling that the whole family was around the table.
Was-- this makes us very-- made us very happy.
My mother spread a-- a white tablecloth on the table.
That the house had to be shiny clean.
We had to work very hard, all of us, the whole day to clean up-- to clean up for Shabbos.
We had to take baths, you know?
Because Shabbos, she didn't like us to-- to bathe.
And that's how, you know, you spend the Shabbos.
Of course, we went to the-- in the evening to theater, to movies, to meet other young people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a lot of friends, that I live very close with them.
I like to read a lot.
I went to the library, I was reading a lot.
In 19-- in 1933, I remember.
I was that time in the-- it was January on the ice skating rink with one of my cousins.
And I remember the radio announcing, the loudspeaker, that Hitler just won.
He won the election.
And it made us right away shiver, that-- that thing.
And then in 1938, the Germans chased out the Polish-- Polish Jews.
The Jews that they weren't the German citizens in Germany, but they were born in Poland.
He chased them back out.
And they didn't have where to live.
They came in masses.
Just the way they stood.
They didn't-- couldn't take with them nothing.
And the Jewish Society announced that every Jew in Warsaw must take in a family.
And we took in a family.
And I had to share with another girl, with a strange girl, my bed.
It was crowded as it was, without them.
So you can imagine the crowd.
But they were our brothers.
And my father said, we must share with them food.
And we must share with them where to live, where to sleep.
Yeah.
That time because we-- we knew that he wrote that book, We knew already that time that he was-- they were very bad to the German Jews, the Nazis.
That they made pogroms.
We knew that they-- they deprived the German Jews of-- of better jobs.
They deprived them of going to school.
We knew that they started with the Jews already at that time.
In Germany.
So we knew that if he's going to have an influence, as it was bad in Poland, we knew that it's going to get even worse, with Hitler's influence.
And that makes us shiver.
We were reading papers.
We were reading Jewish papers, Polish papers, in Polish written.
Our changes began, really, when Hitler came in-- came into Poland.
And this was in 1939, when he-- they marched into Poland.
Actually, we expected the Russians to come in.
The Russians should-- were supposed, but the Russians, as you know, they made a pact with-- with Germany.
And instead of the Russians, we-- we saw the Germans coming in.
And right away, they started, you know, with the Jews.
Right away, as soon as they came in.
We couldn't-- we-- they forbid children to go to school.
My two brothers had to stay home.
They couldn't go to school no more.
We couldn't go to-- to higher-- to get higher education.
We couldn't go to movies, to theaters.
We couldn't even-- we were not allowed to go on the trolley.
Of course, on the-- we had to wear right away armbands with the Jewish star.
Up from 12 years, a child from 12 years had to wear an armband.
To make them-- to-- not-- to-- they should-- it should be visible that he is a Jew for them.
I was caught-- as soon as they came in, I was caught.
I was going in the street.
My mother sent me for something.
And I hear under me yelling, halt, in German.
I turn my head, and I see a German gendarme with a rifle to me.
You know, pointed to me.
So I-- I had to stand.
And they-- they got a group of girls.
And they took us to the park.
In the park, they had those-- there were stationed soldiers.
We had to clean up the toil-- the toilets over there.
We had to clean up the-- the rooms for them.
To-- to-- they should-- that they should be in a clean place.
And I remember I didn't have what to clean and wash the floor.
He said, take off your [GERMAN].
Let me take off your underwear and clean.
We were very unhappy, very sad.
But we still didn't know what the real end was going to be.
We thought-- my father was hoping, telling us, if we be strong enough, we live through.
We live through the bad times, and this won't be forever.
Nobody realized at that time, in 1930, 1940, but that-- that there was going to be a final solution.
That there was going to crematoriums.
This-- we thought we could be strong enough to pull through.
We'll pull through.
In the beginning, there was a chance to leave the country.

The only place where people could leave [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after they marched in, in 1939, beginning of '40, 1940, the-- the only place was to go to cross the border to Russia, which was very difficult.
They had to swim through a-- swim through a river.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father begged me and my sister, we should leave.
But as I was very devoted to my parents, I said, I can't leave and leave you behind.
My whole family couldn't live-- leave, because my mother was not strong enough to make that voyage.
She was sickly.
My younger brother began already to suffer from elevated temperature.
He had a case of glands, enlarged glands.
A matter of fact, that has brought to his death.
We couldn't help him.
And he got sicker and sicker, and he died in the ghetto, a child of 10 years old.
So I was not the one that I could leave them.
I said, I won't be able to live over there, not knowing what's going on with you here.
What's going to happen to you, I'm ready it should happen to me.
And I didn't leave.
Maybe I did wrong.
Because if I would leave, my sister would leave with me, and I-- maybe she would be alive.
And I-- I remained all by myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was shot.
The poor girl was shot from an action in the ghetto, when they were catching in 1942-- they were catching people.
They blockaded the street.
And they took people to send them to Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my sister was already employed.
We all thought that being employed, we're working for them, they let us live.
They won't-- they won't evacuate us to another place.
But this didn't help.
She had-- with the ID in her hand.
She was going from work.
They blockade the streets.
